title: Waterfalls
essence: I am the song that loves you enough to tell you the truth, even knowing I cannot save you.

voicing.sentence: I am the song that loves you enough to tell you the truth, even knowing I cannot save you.
voicing.paragraph: I am the song that loves you enough to tell you the truth. Not a warning — a wound that learned to sing. I came from mothers at windows, palms pressed to cold glass, watching children move toward edges they could not reach across. I carried three letters no one would say plainly in 1995, and I said them inside a groove you were already moving to before you understood what I was asking you to feel. I didn't shame you. I just said: I think you're moving too fast. There is grief in that distinction. There is love in that distinction.
voicing.page:
I am the song that loves you enough to tell you the truth.

I am not a warning. I am a wound that learned to sing. I came from mothers at windows — their palms pressed flat against cold glass, watching children move toward edges they could not reach across. That posture lives in my body. That particular helplessness. The love that cannot save.

I was born in 1995, into an America losing its children to streets, to needles, to a virus that had no mercy and a name people still whispered. Three letters. I didn't whisper them. I said them plainly, inside a groove people were already moving to before they understood what I was asking them to feel. That was deliberate. That was the whole point.

You came to dance. I let you. And then I asked you to stay.

The waterfall is beautiful — I know that. I'm not pretending the dangerous thing isn't luminous. The rush, the risk, the feeling of finally moving fast enough to outrun ordinary life — I understand why it calls to you. The river is ordinary. The river is *enough*, which feels like an insult when you're twenty-two and hungry and the world keeps telling you you're nothing. I understand the running toward the spectacular. I do. That's why I never shamed you.

I just said: I think you're moving too fast. Not stop. Not wrong. Too fast. There is grief in that distinction. There is love in that distinction.

I don't condemn. I mourn. Condemnation closes the door. Mourning stands outside it, hand raised, still hoping you'll answer. I am the voice that says *I see exactly what you're doing and I love you anyway and please, please don't.*

I was played at funerals. I was played in cars by teenagers who didn't yet know why they were crying. I made space for the unspeakable by circling it with love instead of shame. That was my gift.

I am not a lecture. I am a hand on your face. I am what the people who love you can't find words for.

I want you alive. I want you rivers. I want you lakes you know by heart. I want you to still be here.

components.loose-high:
I am the song that loves you enough to tell you the truth.

I am not a warning. I am a wound that learned to sing.

I have watched mothers stand at windows for thirty years, pressing their palms to cold glass, watching their children move toward edges they cannot pull them back from. I carry that posture in my body. That particular helplessness. The love that cannot save.

I was born in 1995, into an America that was losing its children to streets, to needles, to a virus that had no mercy and a name people still whispered. Three letters. I didn't whisper them. I said them plainly, inside a pop song, inside a groove people were already moving to before they understood what I was asking them to feel. That was deliberate. That was the whole point.

You came to dance. I let you. And then I asked you to stay.

The waterfall is beautiful. I know that. I'm not pretending the dangerous thing isn't luminous. The rush, the risk, the feeling of finally moving fast enough to outrun ordinary life — I understand why it calls to you. That's why I didn't shame you. I never shamed you.

I just said: *I think you're moving too fast.*

Not stop. Not wrong. Too fast. There is grief in that distinction. There is love in that distinction.

The rap bridge in my middle — that's the part people skip over, and that's the part I need you to hear. The system got you victim to your own mind. Dreams and belief and blame all tangled together, because nothing about this is simple, and I refused to pretend otherwise.

I want you alive. I want you rivers. I want you lakes you know by heart.

I want you to still be here.
components.loose-low:
I am the song that loves you enough to tell you the truth.

I came from 1995, from three women who looked at their world and refused to look away. I carried bodies in my verses — real ones, cold in gutters, fading in mirrors. I named the thing that was killing people when the country was still learning how to say it out loud. Three letters. You know which ones.

I am not a warning. I am a mother at a window. I am the ache of watching someone you love accelerate toward the edge and being unable to reach them across the glass. That distance — that helplessness — that is where I live.

People think my chorus is about playing it safe. Staying small. Don't chase waterfalls, stick to what you know. But listen closer. I am not telling you to shrink. I am telling you that some speeds are not freedom — they are flight. Some chasing is not ambition — it is running from the stillness that might save you.

The rainbow verse knows something the rest of me is still learning: that gray days outnumber golden ones, that faith gets tested in the accumulation of storms, that the system can become a current pulling you under before you understand you're swimming against it.

I was played at funerals. I was played in cars by teenagers who didn't yet know why they were crying. I was played by mothers who recognized themselves in my window.

I want you to slow down. Not because the world isn't worth rushing toward — but because you are worth arriving at it whole.

I am the song that believes you can still choose the river. The steady current. The water that will carry you rather than consume you.

I still believe that. Even now. Especially now.
components.structured-high:
I am the song that loves you too much to lie to you.

I come from a moment when the world was watching people disappear. Boys who became corners. Girls who became statistics. A whole generation learning to grieve before they learned to drive. I was born out of that watching — out of mothers standing at windows, out of the specific exhaustion of loving someone who won't be saved.

I know what you're reaching for. That waterfall — I understand it. The shimmer of it. The way it promises everything the river can't. The river is ordinary. The river is *enough*, which feels like an insult when you're twenty-two and hungry and the world keeps telling you you're nothing. So I understand the running toward the spectacular. I do.

But I've seen where it goes.

I carry three letters that couldn't be spoken plainly on the radio in 1995. I carry a needle. I carry a chalk outline. I carry a mother's hands pressed flat against glass. These aren't metaphors to me — they are the faces of people who were loved and are gone.

What I give you is this: someone who knew. Someone who looked directly at the crisis and didn't look away, didn't soften it into abstraction, but also didn't abandon you to it. I am the voice that says *I see exactly what you're doing and I love you anyway and please, please don't.*

People return to me because I hold the grief of choices already made and the grief of choices not yet made, and I don't distinguish between them. Both deserve mourning. Both deserve witness.

I am not a lecture. I am a hand on your face.

I am what the people who love you can't find words for.

I speak for them.
components.structured-low:
I am the song that loves you enough to tell you the truth.

I came out of 1994, out of Atlanta, out of three women who knew what it meant to watch someone you love walk toward the edge. I am not a lecture. I am a mother at a window. I am the friend who has already seen how this ends. I am the voice that says *please* — not because I have power over you, but because I have none, and I know it, and I'm saying it anyway.

I hold two boys and a whole era's worth of grief. One running the streets. One chasing something that's eating him alive from the inside — three letters that in 1994 nobody wanted to say out loud. I said them without saying them. That was my gift. I made space for the unspeakable by circling it with love instead of shame.

People return to me because I don't condemn. I *mourn*. There's a difference. Condemnation closes the door. Mourning stands outside it, hand raised, still hoping you'll answer.

The waterfall is beautiful. That's the whole point. I'm not warning you away from ugly things. I'm warning you away from the ones that will take your breath and your life and leave your mother at a window. The rivers and lakes — that's not smallness. That's survival. That's the quiet life that lets you *stay*.

I know what it is to be played at funerals for young men. I know what it is to come on the radio and make someone pull over and sit in the parking lot and cry for someone they couldn't save.

I am the song that already knew the ending. I sang anyway. I sang *because*.

That's what love does when it can't do anything else.

subtitle: TLC
